source: D59555 HAL 4.0 Risk Management Plan rev03.pdf
title: 7.0 Verification Activities > 7.1 Risk control measures
text_content: The three risk control options shall be considered in their order of priority: o Inherently safe design and manufacture; o Protective measures in the device itself or in the manufacturing process; o Information for safety (warnings/precautions/contra-indications) and, where appropriate, training to users. All recognized risk control measures shall be implemented to reduce risk to or maintain risk at an acceptable level. Implementation of risk control measures shall continue until successive risk control options or measures do not further reduce the estimated risk. Whenever possible, requirements of international standards or their corresponding harmonized versions shall be utilized as risk control measures. These applied standards are listed or referenced in Appendix B of this RMP. The implementation of risk control measures shall be verified. The effectiveness of the implemented risk control measures shall be verified or, as appropriate, validated.

title: 3. DAEN > 7.1.8 Conclusions of the Analysis of Manufacturer Data (Continued)
text_content: Mitigations through product design and labeling are in place. The mitigations were derived from thorough risk analysis processes through which risk control measures are formulated, implemented, and validated during product development. The risk control measures have been tested through verification and validation activities, and through product usability studies. The risks and their associated potential hazards have been assessed via analysis of available clinical data regarding the use of HAL. The safety findings in the clinical data were analyzed in context of the benefits to the patients as described above. Clinical data was analyzed for any evidence of the residual risks of HAL 4.0 identified in the RMR report. No adverse events or clinical toxicities were reported as attributable to the residual risks outlined in Section 5.1.4 (e.g. radiation over/under exposure, collision).

title: 9.0 List of Tasks > 9.1 Use-related Risk Analysis
text_content: D56979 HAL 4.0 Risk Management Report identifies safety-related use errors, their design mitigators, and the risk control operator for the design mitigators. Design mitigators where a human is the risk control operator are considered for inclusion in the summative usability testing.  Design mitigators that identify the risk control operators as the device are not included in the summative testing. Hazard-related use scenarios to be included in the summative test were identified from these reports.  The product team selected a subset of the human factor related hazard-related use scenarios based on the severity of the potential harm that could be caused by use error (e.g., for which medical intervention would be needed). During the risk hazard analysis meetings, the team identified which risks have a human factor (HF) cause type. title: 3.2.1 Scope, Schedule and Budget Control
text_content: Scope control will consist of managing requirement changes, enhancements, feature requests and scope changes.  Each will be controlled as outlined below. Requirement changes: If all of these conditions are met:  The changes do not impact the essential functionality and/or user experience of the device  The changed do not impact the program schedule committed to MER by more than two calendar weeks Then the changes will be managed through the document control process using Review Report Forms (LL40). If the above conditions are not all met, then the requirement change is elevated to a change in scope by definition.  See Scope Change section below. Enhancements and Feature Requests: Any requirement changes to implement additional features or enhancements, after Elaboration Phase, will be collected as TFS backlog items reporting to an Enhancement EPIC. text_content: Main control of content for accuracy, completeness, and quality is through reviews, internal and formal; UAD keystroking and tryout of new or changed features and procedures. Internal reviews are uncontrolled. They are intended to: Capture most changes before the formal review with longer review periods than the formal review. Ensure that all project team members have a chance to respond to the draft UA so that GPL can deliver the UA as planned. Resolve and reduce as many findings as possible before formal review. Formal reviews are controlled—findings are recorded—and require participation of the project team per RACI for Design and Development and GPL Authoring Process. For general design quality, see D95424 AL 4.0 MR2 Evolve Maintenance Release Document.

title: 3.0 Definitions > 4.2 Integrity > 4.2.2 Software Integrity:
text_content: During the design and development phases of the lifecycle for the application software within Varian medical devices, the integrity of the system build is ensured by controlling the editing process and requiring regular reviews of the code. This is done to ensure that the integrity of the application software is maintained through the construction phase. W hen the software package is ready for release to the user, Varian manufacturing procedures (DND1902) are used to prevent improper or incompatible versions of the software from being distributed or installed. These procedures include measures to ensure the consistency of data, the absence of viruses or malware, and the inclusion of needed user documentation and labeling. For Varian medical devices, installation procedures and updates are handled by trained Varian Service personnel or local Service representatives. Acceptance procedures are utilized to provide a further barrier to loss of integrity, as the

title: 2.0 Validation Overview > 2.3 Risk Control Measures Validation
text_content: All Risk Control Measures in which the operator is human shall be validated by the validation team of domain experts for their effectiveness. Actors are defined in section 2.1. All Risk Control Measures in which the operator is process shall be validated by Manufacturing and managed by Manufacturing planning documents. Risk Control Measures validation is not in scope for this release. There are no changes affecting safety requirements, labels or warnings that require human interaction. There are no new RCMs introduced as part of HAL 4.0 MR1. All Risk Control Measures that require human interaction were validated with HAL 4.0. For Addition information refer to the MRD section 3 Risk, Hazard, and Safety Analysis (2).

EN ISO 13485: 2016 EN ISO 14971:2019 +A11:2021 The use of the quality system standard EN ISO 13485 assures design and manufacture quality. The risk management standard EN ISO 14971 was followed to evaluate and mitigate risks. HAL has been designed and manufactured in such a way that it can be used safely with the materials, substances and gases with which it contacts during normal use or during routine procedures. HAL does not use medicinal products in its function. The only fluid used is water for cooling. The only gas used is Sulphur Hexafluoride in the wave guides. EN ISO 13485: 2016 EN ISO 14971: 2019 The use of the quality system standard EN ISO 13485 assures design and manufacture quality. The risk management standard EN ISO 14971 was followed to evaluate and mitigate risks. HAL has been designed and manufactured in such a way that it can be used safely with the materials, substances and gases with which it contacts during normal use or during routine procedures. HAL does not use medicinal products in its function. The only fluid used is water for cooling. The only gas used is Sulphur Hexafluoride in the wave guides. Risks from water and Sulphur Hexafluoride are covered in the Risk Management Report. Verification Summary Report: D68588

title: 8.0 Production and Post-production Information
text_content: Production and post-production information will be continually monitored and analyzed to determine if new risks have been identified and to review the assessments of known risks to maintain an effective risk management process. This information includes, but is not limited to, design and process changes, revisions of standards, identifications of defects, and reports of complaints. In addition to Varian’s information about the device, the following shall be reviewed: • Information on similar devices on the market; • Publicly available information such as recalls, FDA Medical Device Reports, and vigilance reports; • Scientific literature. Analysis of relevant data is used to demonstrate that risk-based decisions and risk control measures for this device are appropriate and effective.
